I have a couple of questions on this, and I don't think I have as much context as everybody else in the room, so apologies if these are like the wrong questions or like so obvious that it's something to ask.
But one of my questions is, I'm seeing a lot of us like very focused on the SEO side.
Where does like the technical SEO, I know you walked through like existing content and new content.
How does, like what's the technical product look like?
And like I know that there's point of use on like getting a bunch of really important pages like in the footer.
Or like how involved are you in that side is my first question.
Yeah, so normally what we do, it really depends.
So I'll just tell you like specific examples, right?
Galileo AI, they were literally migrating domains, right?
We are kicking off.
We helped with their entire migration, right?
We had another one, Firework.com.
A lot of their pages weren't getting indexed, so we did a lot of audits and kind of walked them through some of the changes on the site that they needed to make to fix some of that.

One of the issues was essentially their global nav was implemented in a way that Google wasn't able to index it or crawl it, which then meant that Google was only relying on the footer to figure out what pages were important to them, right?
And so it's the way the global nav was rendered, essentially, right?
There's issues where we're seeing where there's a lot of issues with a lot of thin content or content that's extremely outdated or that doesn't cross-link.
There's things around just cross-linking, which is part of our process standard.
But the TLDR to your question is we usually do an audit at the beginning, but then that's an ongoing thing.
So every single week, I wouldn't say we're doing kind of this crazy in-depth audit, but we're looking at signals.
So we're looking at, hey.
So
The content getting indexed?
Are the pages getting indexed?
Or is your website health overall good?
Are there any bad signals or anything that's alarming here?
And outside of completely redesigning your entire site, if there's anything we can help tackle, whether it's going into the CMS and fixing some things.
I'll give you another example.
WebStacks, they're actually a web dev agency, one of our clients.
And one of the things we've done for them is that a lot of their pages actually didn't have meta descriptions and meta titles or had a lot of duplicate ones.
And so we ran a workflow to essentially fix that for hundreds of pages pretty quickly as well, right?
So our goal is not to just publish content.
Content is just like the way to feel like you're getting a real deliverable that actually will drive the right outcomes.
But obviously, if we only publish content and didn't help you fix if there's issues on the SEO side.
But I think also like often when people say technical SEO, people kind of overcomplicate it.
And, you know, like for instance, like Dave.
you, Dave.
I was in charge of SEO for TripAdvisor with 100 million pages.
On that stuff, there's real technical SEO challenges because you have literally hundreds of millions of pages, and you have to figure out how pages are dynamically generated.
There's all kinds of issues with sites of hundreds or a few thousand pages.
The technical things are usually pretty common things, or usually around the experience of the pages, navigation, cross-linking, how rich the pages are.
How often they're getting refreshed and improved, the quality of the content, and then it comes out to a lot of things around, like, are people linking to you and to your domain?
Is your domain authoritative?
So we do some link-building work streams as well for companies that have newer domains.
And so that's why the first 60 days is a little bit of setting expectations, setting the strategy, making sure we both want to work together long-term, right?
And then, worst-case scenario, you know, at the end of the

You have this amazing roadmap that you can get a lot of value from and execute against, as well as like, or if we choose to continue to work together, then, you know, we have, we can start to execute really, really quickly as well.
Cool.
My other question is, do you, outside of content creation, do you support distribution of content?
Do you, like, what is, or does it kind of like stop at the content creation and publishing?
We do.
It's really, like, within the first 60 days, we tend to not try to add on up front, right?
Because it tends to be a little bit wasteful, to be honest.

Like, if you try to do five different work streams in the first 60 days, what's going to end up happening is, like, we're still going through the same process, whether it's, like, one work stream or three work streams, because we need to learn about you, your audience, set the right strategy, get alignment, what does, you know, done look like for you, right?
Like, for different companies, completely different.
Like, him and hers, we're talking to them, and they wanted a doctor to review every piece of content.
That's very different.
Right.
Process of what done looks like, you know, versus like even abnormal security, their bar is very different as well.
The way we do fact checking is very different than RAM, for example.
Right.
And so from that, like then we can add on.
So, for instance, like Galileo, we do podcast content repurposing.
We've done like newsletters or long form content where we're interviewing customers for you, creating case studies.
There's a lot of things we can bolt on.
